metadata.title: Build a Four-Verb Career Signal Audit with Claude
metadata.purpose: Show students in Nina Harris' Branding and AI course how to build a four-verb portfolio self-audit that scores each project on Ideate/Build/Brand/Ship and reveals which verbs are weakest in the portfolio.
metadata.audience: Students and practitioners in a university branding course with an AI component
sources: Branding and AI, Chapter 1 — The Creative Engineer (Nina Harris)

skin_warnings: B00: palette=claude but the cold open is 'ClaudeComposerAsk916' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
END: palette=claude but the outro is 'un-annotated' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

metadata.reformat: center-cut, focus-aware; pantry/<bid>-916.* is the human replacement slot; -916 overrides honored

beats.narration_text: Annyeong — this is Liam, in for Bear. For Nina Harris' course, Branding and AI, here is the question we are running today: generative tools are excellent at producing solutions. Given a specification, they will build it. What they cannot do is decide whether the specification is worth building — and that is the verb that still carries career value. We are building the audit that shows you which verbs your portfolio is missing.

props.command: Four-verb portfolio audit (Ideate/Build/Brand/Ship). Score these three projects 0–3 per verb: [PROJECT 1], [PROJECT 2], [PROJECT 3]. Aggregate verb profile. For the lowest-scoring verb, recommend one next project type.
output: Build: 9/9 — maxed across all three projects

beats.narration_text: Here is what breaks without this audit. You have three strong GitHub repos. You built all of them — technically solid, well-documented, properly tested. The market looks at your portfolio and reads one verb: Build. They cannot read Ideate from a repo, because the repo does not show whether you chose the problem or were handed a spec. They cannot read Ship, because there is no evidence of real users. Build was the separating signal. Now it is not.

props.sparkLine: Build was the signal. Now it's noise.

beats.narration_text: The theory from Chapter One. The Creative Engineer framework: four verbs every practitioner needs to demonstrate across their portfolio. Ideate — did they identify the problem independently, or were they handed a specification? Build — is the technical artifact present and functional? Brand — is there a named, positioned identity with a clear audience? Ship — did it reach real users with a feedback loop? Chapter One's explicit failure mode: students pick a technology, build around it, then try to retrofit a user need. The Build verb is AI-cheapened. The Ideate and Ship verbs are not.

props.recommendation: Ship: build one project that requires talking to real users before writing any code.

beats.narration_text: The centerpiece: this is the scorecard for a portfolio of three AI projects — all technically implemented GitHub repos with no user testing and no named brand identity. Rows: ML GitHub Repo, AI Dashboard, Data Pipeline. Columns: Ideate, Build, Brand, Ship scored zero to three. Build maxes at three on all three projects — nine of nine. Ideate averages one. Brand scores one total. Ship scores one total, highlighted in terracotta as the weakest verb. The recommendation below: a project that requires talking to real users before writing a single line of code. Source: Branding and AI, Nina Harris, Chapter One.

props.recommendation: Ship: build one project that requires talking to real users before writing any code.

beats.narration_text: Honesty about what the audit does not do. Build does not score zero for AI-assisted projects. The verb is present even if cheapened — the audit measures profile balance, not whether AI helped. A portfolio with four strong Ship projects and weak Build scores is still unbalanced. The goal is all four verbs present, not the obsolete ones scored out.

artifactLines: Build is not scored zero for AI-assisted projects — it is still present.
The audit measures profile balance, not whether AI helped.
A portfolio heavy in Ship but weak in Build is still unbalanced.
The goal is all four verbs present, not the cheapened ones scored out.

beats.narration_text: Let us recap with Claude. Four-Verb Career Signal Audit. Four lines.

artifactLines: Build is the cheapened verb. Ideate and Ship are the costly signals that still separate.
The scorecard surfaces which verbs the portfolio demonstrates and which it hides.
The lowest-scoring verb gets a specific next-project recommendation.
The audit forces you to name the gap before the market names it for you.

beats.narration_text: That's the short version. The full video also covers four-verb career audit — watch Build a Four-Verb Career Signal Audit with Claude for the whole story. The link is right below.
